title: Find Customers With Consecutive Purchases
solution:
--result should contain customer_id, purchase_start_date, purchase_end_date, total_amount

select * from purchases_info
order by customer_id asc, purchase_date asc

with lead_purchase as(
select purchase_id,
		customer_id,
		purchase_date,
		amount,
		lead(purchase_date,1,null) over(partition by customer_id order by purchase_date asc) as lead_purchase_date,
		lead(amount,1,null) over(partition by customer_id order by purchase_date asc) as lead_purchase_amount,
		datediff(day,purchase_date,lead(purchase_date,1,null) over(partition by customer_id order by purchase_date asc)) as diff_date
from purchases_info
where amount > 100
), base_data as(
select distinct customer_id,
		min(purchase_date) over(partition by customer_id) as purchase_start_date,
		first_value(amount) over(partition by customer_id order by purchase_date asc) as purchase_amount,
		max(lead_purchase_date) over(partition by customer_id) as purchase_end_date,
		sum(lead_purchase_amount) over(partition by customer_id) as lead_purchase_amount
from lead_purchase
where diff_date = 1
)
select customer_id,
		purchase_start_date,
		purchase_end_date,
		(purchase_amount + lead_purchase_amount) as total_purchase_amount
from base_data;

title: Calculate The Average Subscription Duration, Subscription Gap And The Average Amount Paid Per Subscription For Each Customer
solution:
--select * from Subscriptions;

with t1 as(
select *,
		datediff(day,start_date,end_date) as duration,
		lag(end_date,1,null) over(partition by customer_id order by end_date asc) as pre_subs_end_date
from subscriptions
), t2 as(
select *,
		datediff(day,pre_subs_end_date,start_date) as subscription_gap
from t1
)
select customer_id, 
		avg(duration) as average_subscription_duration,
		avg(amount_paid) as average_amount_paid,
		max(subscription_gap) as longest_gap_between_subscriptions
from t2
group by customer_id
having count(subscription_id) > 2
order by longest_gap_between_subscriptions desc;
question:

Additionally, identify customers who have a longest subscription gap between two consecutive subscriptions.

	>customer_id
	>average_subscription_duration (in days)
	>average_amount_paid (average amount paid per subscription)
	>longest_gap_between_subscriptions (the longest gap in days between two consecutive subscriptions for each customer)

2.Calculate the average amount paid as the average of the amount_paid column for each customer.
3.For each customer, find the longest gap between two consecutive subscriptions by 
	calculating the difference between the start_date of a current subscription

5.Return the results ordered by longest_gap_between_subscriptions in descending order.

title: Most Streamed Songs Overall, Regardless Of The Subscription Type
schema: Song_Streams(stream_id, song_id, song_name, artist_name, genre, user_id, subscription_type, stream_date)
solution:
--select * from Song_Streams;


with top_three as(
select top 3 song_id,
		count(stream_id) as stream_count
from song_streams
group by song_id
order by stream_count desc
), t2 as (
select song_id,
		sum(case when subscription_type = 'Premium' then 1 else 0 end) as pre_sub_count,
		sum(case when subscription_type = 'Free' then 1 else 0 end) as fre_sub_count,
		count(stream_id) as stream_count
from 
song_streams
where song_id in (select distinct song_id from top_three)
group by song_id
)
select song_id,
		cast((100.0*pre_sub_count/stream_count) as decimal(8,2)) as premium_percent,
		cast((100.0*fre_sub_count/stream_count) as decimal(8,2)) as fre_percent,
		case when pre_sub_count > fre_sub_count then 'Premium'
			when pre_sub_count < fre_sub_count then 'Free'
			else 'Both' end as most_streamed_sub,
			stream_count
from t2
order by stream_count desc;

title: RFM Tier Segmentation — Tier 1 (Top 10%), Tier 2, Tier 3 Repeat Customers
scenario:
An online store wants a tiered loyalty programme grounded in customer growth accounting. Each repeat customer is bucketed using the classic RFM trio (Recency, Frequency, Monetary). The business rule:
  • Tier 1 = top 10 % by composite RFM score (the white-glove cohort)
  • Tier 2 = next 15 % (high-value but not VIP)
  • Tier 3 = the remainder of repeat customers
One-time buyers (frequency = 1) are excluded — they're handled by the first-purchase activation campaign, not the loyalty programme.
question:
From `orders` (only rows where `status = 'paid'`), compute per customer:
  • recency_days  — days from last order to snapshot date 2025-04-01
                    (lower = better)
  • frequency     — lifetime paid order count (higher = better)
  • monetary      — lifetime spend (higher = better)
  • rfm_pct       — composite percentile rank in [0, 1] where 1.0 = best.
                    Compute it as the average of three PERCENT_RANK()
                    values — recency (DESC because lower days is better),
                    frequency (ASC), and monetary (ASC).
  • tier          — 'Tier 1' if rfm_pct >= 0.90,
                    'Tier 2' if rfm_pct >= 0.75,
                    else 'Tier 3'

Exclude one-time customers (frequency = 1). Sort by tier ASC, rfm_pct DESC.
solution:
-- Setup — canonical orders table.
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS orders;
CREATE TABLE orders (
    order_id    INTEGER PRIMARY KEY,
    customer_id INTEGER NOT NULL,
    order_date  TEXT    NOT NULL,
    amount      REAL    NOT NULL,
    status      TEXT    NOT NULL
);
INSERT INTO orders VALUES
  -- C1 — frequent, recent, big spender (Tier 1 candidate)
  (1,  1, '2024-11-10',  420.00, 'paid'),
  (2,  1, '2025-01-15',  680.00, 'paid'),
  (3,  1, '2025-02-20',  510.00, 'paid'),
  (4,  1, '2025-03-30',  990.00, 'paid'),
  -- C2 — moderate (Tier 2 candidate)
  (5,  2, '2024-08-01',  150.00, 'paid'),
  (6,  2, '2024-12-12',  240.00, 'paid'),
  (7,  2, '2025-02-08',  300.00, 'paid'),
  -- C3 — long lapsed but lots of orders (Tier 3 — low recency)
  (8,  3, '2024-01-05',   70.00, 'paid'),
  (9,  3, '2024-02-12',   85.00, 'paid'),
  (10, 3, '2024-03-20',   95.00, 'paid'),
  -- C4 — one-timer, EXCLUDED (frequency = 1)
  (11, 4, '2025-03-01',  180.00, 'paid'),
  -- C5 — small recent (Tier 3 — low frequency / low monetary)
  (12, 5, '2025-02-15',   60.00, 'paid'),
  (13, 5, '2025-03-15',   80.00, 'paid'),
  -- C6 — refunded order, EXCLUDED at WHERE clause
  (14, 6, '2025-03-30',  500.00, 'refunded');

-- Solution — RFM with PERCENT_RANK, then top-10 / next-15 / rest split.
WITH paid AS (
    SELECT customer_id, order_date, amount
    FROM orders
    WHERE status = 'paid'
),
agg AS (
    SELECT
        customer_id,
        CAST(julianday('2025-04-01') - julianday(MAX(order_date)) AS INTEGER)
            AS recency_days,
        COUNT(*)              AS frequency,
        ROUND(SUM(amount), 2) AS monetary
    FROM paid
    GROUP BY customer_id
    HAVING COUNT(*) > 1
),
scored AS (
    SELECT *,
        PERCENT_RANK() OVER (ORDER BY recency_days DESC) AS r_pct,
        PERCENT_RANK() OVER (ORDER BY frequency    ASC)  AS f_pct,
        PERCENT_RANK() OVER (ORDER BY monetary     ASC)  AS m_pct
    FROM agg
)
SELECT
    customer_id,
    recency_days,
    frequency,
    monetary,
    ROUND((r_pct + f_pct + m_pct) / 3.0, 3) AS rfm_pct,
    CASE
        WHEN (r_pct + f_pct + m_pct) / 3.0 >= 0.90 THEN 'Tier 1'
        WHEN (r_pct + f_pct + m_pct) / 3.0 >= 0.75 THEN 'Tier 2'
        ELSE 'Tier 3'
    END AS tier
FROM scored
ORDER BY tier ASC, rfm_pct DESC;

title: Long-Standing Loyal Customers — Two-Year Active Streak
scenario: Marketing wants to identify long-standing customers — people who have been steadily shopping for at least two years AND placed orders in at least 12 distinct calendar months. These are the backbone of the store's revenue and the natural target of a 'thank-you' campaign.
question:
Return one row per qualifying customer with:
  • customer_id
  • first_order_date
  • last_order_date
  • months_active   — distinct YYYY-MM with at least one order
  • lifetime_orders
  • lifetime_spend

Qualifying rules:
  • last_order_date - first_order_date >= 730 days (2 years), AND
  • months_active >= 12

Sort by lifetime_spend DESC.
solution:
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS orders;
CREATE TABLE orders (
    order_id    INTEGER PRIMARY KEY,
    customer_id INTEGER NOT NULL,
    order_date  TEXT    NOT NULL,
    amount      REAL    NOT NULL
);
INSERT INTO orders VALUES
  -- C1 — qualifies: 2022-01 → 2024-06, 13 distinct months
  (1, 1, '2022-01-15', 100), (2, 1, '2022-04-10', 80),
  (3, 1, '2022-07-21', 60), (4, 1, '2022-10-05', 90),
  (5, 1, '2023-02-12', 110), (6, 1, '2023-05-18', 130),
  (7, 1, '2023-08-22', 120), (8, 1, '2023-11-30', 200),
  (9, 1, '2024-01-05', 150), (10, 1, '2024-02-09', 90),
  (11, 1, '2024-03-14', 60), (12, 1, '2024-05-20', 70),
  (13, 1, '2024-06-25', 50),
  -- C2 — long timespan but only 4 active months → fails
  (14, 2, '2022-03-01', 50), (15, 2, '2023-04-01', 50),
  (16, 2, '2024-05-01', 50), (17, 2, '2024-06-01', 50),
  -- C3 — many months but only 8 months between first/last → fails
  (18, 3, '2024-01-05', 80), (19, 3, '2024-02-09', 90),
  (20, 3, '2024-03-14', 60), (21, 3, '2024-05-20', 70),
  (22, 3, '2024-06-25', 50), (23, 3, '2024-07-25', 70),
  (24, 3, '2024-08-25', 80);

SELECT
    customer_id,
    MIN(order_date) AS first_order_date,
    MAX(order_date) AS last_order_date,
    COUNT(DISTINCT substr(order_date, 1, 7)) AS months_active,
    COUNT(*)        AS lifetime_orders,
    ROUND(SUM(amount), 2) AS lifetime_spend
FROM orders
GROUP BY customer_id
HAVING (julianday(MAX(order_date)) - julianday(MIN(order_date))) >= 730
   AND COUNT(DISTINCT substr(order_date, 1, 7)) >= 12
ORDER BY lifetime_spend DESC;

title: Customer Growth Accounting — New, Resurrected, Existing, Churned
scenario:
Customer growth accounting (Stripe / Jeff Sauro framework) decomposes every active customer in a given month into one of four buckets so the team can read the health of the business from a single table:
  • New        — first ever order this month
  • Resurrected — active this month, active in some prior month, but NOT last month
  • Existing   — active this month AND active last month
  • Churned    — NOT active this month, but active last month (counted negatively)

The Quick Ratio = (New + Resurrected) / Churned. > 1 means the store is growing.
question:
Produce one row per (year_month, bucket) with:
  year_month  | bucket       | customers
where bucket ∈ ('new', 'resurrected', 'existing', 'churned'). Customers count is positive for new/resurrected/existing, and we report churned as a separate positive number (the consumer can negate it).
solution:
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS orders;
CREATE TABLE orders (
    order_id    INTEGER PRIMARY KEY,
    customer_id INTEGER NOT NULL,
    order_date  TEXT    NOT NULL,
    amount      REAL    NOT NULL
);
INSERT INTO orders VALUES
  (1, 1, '2025-01-05', 100), (2, 1, '2025-02-08', 100),
  (3, 1, '2025-03-12', 100),                          -- C1 New, Existing, Existing
  (4, 2, '2025-01-20', 80),  (5, 2, '2025-03-15', 80), -- C2 New, [skip Feb], Resurrected in Mar
  (6, 3, '2025-02-18', 50),                            -- C3 New in Feb, Churned in Mar
  (7, 4, '2025-03-22', 200);                           -- C4 New in Mar

WITH monthly AS (
    SELECT DISTINCT customer_id, substr(order_date, 1, 7) AS ym
    FROM orders
),
first_seen AS (
    SELECT customer_id, MIN(ym) AS first_ym FROM monthly GROUP BY customer_id
),
labeled AS (
    SELECT
        m.ym,
        m.customer_id,
        CASE
            WHEN m.ym = fs.first_ym THEN 'new'
            WHEN EXISTS (
                SELECT 1 FROM monthly p
                WHERE p.customer_id = m.customer_id
                  AND p.ym = strftime('%Y-%m', date(m.ym || '-01', '-1 month'))
            ) THEN 'existing'
            ELSE 'resurrected'
        END AS bucket
    FROM monthly m
    JOIN first_seen fs ON fs.customer_id = m.customer_id
),
active_months AS (
    SELECT DISTINCT ym FROM monthly
),
churn AS (
    SELECT
        am.ym,
        m.customer_id,
        'churned' AS bucket
    FROM active_months am
    JOIN monthly m
      ON m.ym = strftime('%Y-%m', date(am.ym || '-01', '-1 month'))
    LEFT JOIN monthly cur
      ON cur.customer_id = m.customer_id AND cur.ym = am.ym
    WHERE cur.customer_id IS NULL
)
SELECT ym AS year_month, bucket, COUNT(DISTINCT customer_id) AS customers
FROM (
    SELECT ym, customer_id, bucket FROM labeled
    UNION ALL
    SELECT ym, customer_id, bucket FROM churn
)
GROUP BY ym, bucket
ORDER BY ym,
    CASE bucket WHEN 'new' THEN 1 WHEN 'resurrected' THEN 2
                WHEN 'existing' THEN 3 ELSE 4 END;

title: Resurrected Customers — 90-Day Gap Then a Brand-New Order
scenario: A 'resurrected' customer (per the customer-growth-accounting framework) is one who had a gap of MORE than 90 days since their last order and then placed a new order. The single fresh order is enough to count them as resurrected — they're the natural target of a thank-you-for-coming-back coupon, ahead of any active or fully churned customer.
question:
Identify every resurrection event — a customer order whose immediately previous order is strictly MORE than 90 days earlier:

  customer_id | idle_start | comeback_date | gap_days | comeback_amount

Return one row per such event (a customer with multiple resurrection cycles can appear multiple times). Sort by comeback_date ASC.
solution:
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS orders;
CREATE TABLE orders (
    order_id    INTEGER PRIMARY KEY,
    customer_id INTEGER NOT NULL,
    order_date  TEXT    NOT NULL,
    amount      REAL    NOT NULL
);
INSERT INTO orders VALUES
  -- C1 — idle Jan→May (124d) → RESURRECTED on 2024-05-13
  (1, 1, '2024-01-10', 200), (2, 1, '2024-05-13', 220),
  (3, 1, '2024-05-25', 180), (4, 1, '2024-06-04', 240),
  -- C2 — single follow-up after a 134d gap → still RESURRECTED
  (5, 2, '2024-02-01', 100), (6, 2, '2024-06-15', 110),
  -- C3 — never idle → NOT resurrected
  (7, 3, '2024-01-01', 90),  (8, 3, '2024-02-15', 90),
  (9, 3, '2024-03-20', 90),
  -- C4 — resurrected on 2024-08-10 after 191d gap; ALSO
  -- another 90-day-exact gap on 2024-09-25 (boundary, NOT resurrected)
  (10, 4, '2024-02-01', 50), (11, 4, '2024-08-10', 60),
  (12, 4, '2024-08-30', 70), (13, 4, '2024-11-28', 90);

WITH gaps AS (
    SELECT
        order_id, customer_id, order_date, amount,
        LAG(order_date) OVER (PARTITION BY customer_id ORDER BY order_date)
            AS prev_date
    FROM orders
)
SELECT
    customer_id,
    prev_date AS idle_start,
    order_date AS comeback_date,
    CAST(julianday(order_date) - julianday(prev_date) AS INTEGER) AS gap_days,
    amount AS comeback_amount
FROM gaps
WHERE prev_date IS NOT NULL
  AND julianday(order_date) - julianday(prev_date) > 90
ORDER BY comeback_date;

title: Fraud Signal — Return-to-Purchase Ratio Above 40 %
schema: orders(order_id, customer_id, order_date, amount, status), returns(return_id, order_id, return_date, refund_amount, reason)
scenario: Risk-Ops uses a simple, hard rule for refund fraud screening: any customer whose Return-to-Purchase Ratio exceeds 40 % is automatically flagged for human review. Return-to-Purchase Ratio = returned_orders / total_orders. To rule out statistical noise the rule only applies to customers with at least 5 lifetime orders; smaller histories are labelled 'low_volume'. Analysts also want the refund-to-spend ratio shown alongside so they can see WHY the ratio is high.
question:
Per customer, return:
  customer_id, total_orders, returned_orders, return_to_purchase_ratio,
  total_spend, total_refunded, refund_to_spend_ratio, fraud_flag

fraud_flag rule:
  • 'low_volume'      if total_orders < 5
  • 'high_return_rate' if return_to_purchase_ratio > 0.40
  • else               'clean'

Order by return_to_purchase_ratio DESC, then customer_id ASC.
solution:
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS orders;
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS returns;
CREATE TABLE orders (
    order_id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY,
    customer_id INTEGER, order_date TEXT, amount REAL, status TEXT
);
CREATE TABLE returns (
    return_id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY,
    order_id INTEGER, return_date TEXT,
    refund_amount REAL, reason TEXT
);
INSERT INTO orders VALUES
  -- C1 — 6 orders, 5 returned (0.83) → HIGH_RETURN_RATE
  (1, 1, '2025-03-01', 100, 'paid'), (2, 1, '2025-03-04', 120, 'paid'),
  (3, 1, '2025-03-08', 90, 'paid'),  (4, 1, '2025-03-11', 200, 'paid'),
  (5, 1, '2025-03-15', 80, 'paid'),  (6, 1, '2025-03-18', 110, 'paid'),
  -- C2 — 5 orders, 2 returned (0.40 — boundary, NOT > 0.40) → CLEAN
  (7, 2, '2025-03-01', 100, 'paid'), (8, 2, '2025-03-03', 200, 'paid'),
  (9, 2, '2025-03-05', 800, 'paid'), (10, 2, '2025-03-07', 50, 'paid'),
  (11, 2, '2025-03-09', 50, 'paid'),
  -- C3 — 5 orders, 0 returns → CLEAN
  (12, 3, '2025-03-01', 100, 'paid'), (13, 3, '2025-03-03', 200, 'paid'),
  (14, 3, '2025-03-05', 150, 'paid'), (15, 3, '2025-03-07', 50, 'paid'),
  (16, 3, '2025-03-09', 50, 'paid'),
  -- C4 — only 3 orders, → LOW_VOLUME (under min sample)
  (17, 4, '2025-03-01', 100, 'paid'), (18, 4, '2025-03-03', 100, 'paid'),
  (19, 4, '2025-03-05', 100, 'paid'),
  -- C5 — 5 orders, 3 returned (0.60) → HIGH_RETURN_RATE
  (20, 5, '2025-03-01', 100, 'paid'), (21, 5, '2025-03-03', 100, 'paid'),
  (22, 5, '2025-03-05', 100, 'paid'), (23, 5, '2025-03-07', 100, 'paid'),
  (24, 5, '2025-03-09', 100, 'paid');
INSERT INTO returns VALUES
  (1, 1, '2025-03-05', 100, 'wrong size'), (2, 2, '2025-03-09', 120, 'damaged'),
  (3, 3, '2025-03-12', 90, 'didn''t fit'), (4, 4, '2025-03-15', 200, 'changed mind'),
  (5, 5, '2025-03-20', 80, 'wrong size'),
  -- C2: 2 returns out of 5
  (6, 7, '2025-03-08', 100, 'changed mind'), (7, 8, '2025-03-10', 200, 'damaged'),
  -- C5: 3 returns out of 5
  (8, 20, '2025-03-08', 100, 'wrong size'), (9, 21, '2025-03-10', 100, 'damaged'),
  (10, 22, '2025-03-12', 100, 'changed mind');

WITH per_customer AS (
    SELECT
        o.customer_id,
        COUNT(DISTINCT o.order_id)        AS total_orders,
        COUNT(DISTINCT r.order_id)        AS returned_orders,
        SUM(o.amount)                     AS total_spend,
        COALESCE(SUM(r.refund_amount), 0) AS total_refunded
    FROM orders o
    LEFT JOIN returns r ON r.order_id = o.order_id
    GROUP BY o.customer_id
)
SELECT
    customer_id,
    total_orders,
    returned_orders,
    ROUND(1.0 * returned_orders / total_orders, 2) AS return_to_purchase_ratio,
    total_spend,
    total_refunded,
    ROUND(total_refunded / total_spend, 2) AS refund_to_spend_ratio,
    CASE
      WHEN total_orders < 5 THEN 'low_volume'
      WHEN 1.0 * returned_orders / total_orders > 0.40 THEN 'high_return_rate'
      ELSE 'clean'
    END AS fraud_flag
FROM per_customer
ORDER BY return_to_purchase_ratio DESC, customer_id ASC;

title: Fraud Signal — More Than 3 Shipping Addresses In a 30-Day Window
scenario: A common account-takeover or reshipping-mule fraud pattern is a single account shipping orders to many distinct physical addresses in a short timeframe. Risk wants a daily report of every customer who used MORE than 3 distinct shipping addresses inside any rolling 30-day window — a hard cutoff that goes straight to manual review.
question:
Return one row per offending customer:
  customer_id | window_start | window_end | distinct_addresses | total_amount

where the window is the first 30-day stretch in which the customer's distinct shipping-address count exceeds 3 (i.e. ≥ 4 distinct addresses). Order by distinct_addresses DESC, then by customer_id ASC.
solution:
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS orders;
CREATE TABLE orders (
    order_id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY, customer_id INTEGER,
    order_ts TEXT, amount REAL, shipping_address TEXT
);
INSERT INTO orders VALUES

  (1, 1, '2025-04-01 09:00:00', 200, '1 Pine St'),
